These purchases are particularly noteworthy for two reasons.

First, the largest purchase came from founder and executive chairman Thomas Bradbury, who bought over 36,000 shares at $19.32, investing more than $700,000 of his own capital.

I note that Mr. Bradbury has about five decades of successful track record in operating homebuilders. Notably, he displayed a contrarian streak earlier in his career when, as reported here, he sold his previous venture, Colony Homes, to KB Home in 2003—when the housing market was as hot as the summer in Las Vegas. Five years later, he founded Smith Douglas in Atlanta—when the housing market was as cold as a Minnesota winter—as reported here.

Second, these insiders were buying shares between roughly $16.80 and $19.32, significantly above the current market price.

While insider buying is never a guarantee of future stock performance, it often reflects their confidence in the company’s long-term prospects and/or judgment that the intrinsic value of the shares exceeds their current market price.

The Balance Sheet Remains Strong

Another important factor supporting my upgrade is the company’s conservative balance sheet. According to the latest earnings call, Smith Douglas ended the year with a debt-to-book capitalization of roughly 9% net debt to capitalization of only about 6.6%.

In a cyclical industry like homebuilding, balance sheet strength is crucial. Builders with excessive leverage often struggle even to survive during severe and/or prolonged downturns (which I cannot rule out), while those with conservative balance sheets are better positioned to endure weak demand and capitalize on opportunities when the cycle turns. Smith Douglas clearly belongs to the latter category.

The Stock Price Has Reset

A significant change since my previous article is the stock price. When I originally rated Smith Douglas a Buy, the stock traded around $17 per share. Since then, the price has declined to around $12, representing a substantial contraction in valuation.

While near-term margins may remain under pressure due to the risk factors I described in detail below, the company’s long-term growth strategy—expanding community count, gaining market share, and leveraging its fast construction cycle—remains intact.

Risks to My Contrarian Bullish Thesis

While I am upgrading Smith Douglas to Strong Buy due to the sharp decline in its stock price and the continued strength of its operating model, investors should remain mindful of the apparent risks that could challenge this contrarian thesis.

First, the company’s profitability has deteriorated significantly over the past year. Gross margins declined from roughly 25.5% to 19.9% in the fourth quarter, and full-year margins also compressed meaningfully as the company relied on incentives and closing cost assistance to maintain sales pace in a difficult affordability environment.

Second, management has clearly adopted a “pace over price” operating philosophy, prioritizing sales velocity and inventory turns over short-term pricing discipline. While this strategy may help preserve market share and keep the production engine running, it also means that margins may remain under pressure for an extended period if housing demand does not recover.

Third, land costs remain a potential headwind. Management indicated that some higher land costs from prior deals will continue flowing through the income statement in 2026. This creates the possibility of rising input costs occurring at the same time that selling prices remain under pressure.

Fourth, the last but probably most important, the broader housing cycle remains uncertain. Mortgage rates, employment trends, and consumer confidence will continue to play a critical role in determining housing demand. The close to $40 trillion federal debt, as well as mild but persistent inflationary pressure, makes it rather unlikely that mortgage rates can come down significantly, if at all, to help the housing market recover. If macroeconomic conditions deteriorate further, the housing downturn could persist longer than currently anticipated.

In short, while I believe the recent decline in the stock price has created an attractive entry point, Smith Douglas remains exposed to the cyclical risks inherent in the homebuilding industry. Investors should therefore expect continued volatility in both earnings and share price as the housing cycle evolves.

Therefore, consistent with what I indicated in my prior article, investors who are confident about the long-term prospects of Smith Douglas should continue to implement the dollar-cost-averaging approach over the coming months and years instead of putting all of their capital in by assuming that the stock price has bottomed.

Conclusion

Smith Douglas is clearly operating in a challenging housing environment. Mortgage rates remain elevated, affordability pressures persist, and the company has had to rely more heavily on incentives to maintain the sales pace. These conditions have compressed margins and caused a meaningful decline in earnings over the past year.

The key question for investors is not whether the housing cycle is currently weak—it clearly is—but whether Smith Douglas remains a well-run business capable of compounding value over the long term. Based on the evidence discussed above, I am reasonably confident that the answer remains yes.

The U.S. stock market today sometimes behaves as if it suffers from bipolar disorder. On one side are the “Haves”—the AI and semiconductor sectors—where enthusiasm feels as scorching as Las Vegas in the middle of summer. On the other side are industries like homebuilders and housing-related companies, where sentiment has turned so negative that valuations feel almost frozen in cyclical winter.

But the stock market, like the economy itself, moves in cycles—not in straight lines. Peter Lynch famously liked to buy good companies when they temporarily fell out of favor. Personally, I approach the market in a similar spirit. Just as I prefer to buy artificial Christmas trees right after the holiday season, when nobody wants them, I also like to buy shares of good businesses when they become unpopular. The fact that the business fundamentals of Smith Douglas appear largely intact, while its stock price has reset materially due to such "seasonal" factors, should work in favor of a long-term investor.

Based upon the foregoing, I am upgrading Smith Douglas to Strong Buy.

data.title: Whirlpool Is Transitioning From Major Headwinds To Major Tailwinds
data.description:
I have written a number of articles in Seeking Alpha over the years about turnarounds. It has been my most successful strategy, though finding good ones is difficult. I usually like to see signs of the turnaround in process before I invest. But sometimes, I don’t wait if strong catalysts are in place. That is the case with Whirlpool Corporation (WHR).

Companies with beaten-down stocks tend to be that way for one of five reasons: recession, industry decline, non-recurring issues, poor management, or cyclicality. Whirlpool’s stock is down for 2 of those reasons: non-recurring issues and cyclicality. It has been a double whammy. The non-recurring issue is tariffs, which Whirlpool was expected to benefit from and should become a significant tailwind shortly. But initially, it has been a major headwind. The cyclicality is in their largest markets, new and existing home sales. Whirlpool's stock was further hit by management decisions to cut the dividend in half last summer and a recent dilutive stock offering. Those should also be non-recurring.

In this article, I will detail a number of catalysts and tailwinds in addition to tariffs and an eventual recovery in the housing market. But make no mistake, those two alone could more than double the stock.

Background

Whirlpool is the largest manufacturer and seller of major appliances in North America. Its primary brands in the U.S. are Whirlpool, Jenn-Air, Maytag, KitchenAid, and Amana. It manufactures 80% of what it sells in the U.S. domestically. It also owns InSinkErator (under-sink food disposal), which has a dominant 70% market share in the U.S. and 31% globally.

InSinkErator was acquired in November 2022 for $3.0 billion. In April 2024, Whirlpool sold its European operations for a loss totaling $1.9 billion. After these transactions, Whirlpool was left with a highly leveraged balance sheet just as earnings were dropping from COVID-era highs.

In February 2026, Whirlpool issued $1 billion of new common and preferred stock. The preferred, which totaled $525 million, pays an 8.50% coupon. It automatically converts to common stock at between $69 and $81, depending on the common stock price in 2029, if not converted earlier. The total dilution to common stock is about 24%. Management did the transaction to reduce leverage and provide future flexibility.

The company is somewhat seasonal, with the strongest quarters being the third and fourth. In August 2025, Whirlpool cut its quarterly dividend amount from $1.75 to $0.90. The dividend currently yields 6.2%. The only insider activity in the past 3 months not involving restricted stock was an open market purchase of $198,800 of stock by Director Creed on March 3rd.

Financials

Financial results for the last 5 years, plus guidance for 2026 are shown below.

As shown above, Whirlpool had some of its strongest years ever in 2021 through 2023. Those years were at the top of the cycle and driven by Covid, when people focused on their homes. Before Covid, Whirlpool was up and down and was impacted by cheap imports.

Whirlpool was unable to take advantage of its peak earning years as tangible net worth declined into negative territory and interest-bearing debt increased. This was primarily due to a large loss on the sale of their European business, and massive goodwill and intangible assets were added when acquiring InSinkErator. Revenues declined over the past 5 years in part because the InSinkErator business has less revenue than the European business it replaced.

Revenues and earnings in 2025 were significantly impacted by tariffs. While the tariffs were supposed to help domestic manufacturers like Whirlpool in the short term, they were a big headwind for two reasons. Firstly, major competitors frontloaded massive inventory into the U.S. ahead of the tariffs fully taking effect. This created an oversupply of product, resulting in fewer sales for Whirlpool and lower prices. Tariffs also increased Whirlpool’s cost of components and some finished products by $300 million in 2025.

Guidance

The 2026 guidance was recently adjusted for the stock issuance dilution, and 2026 EPS was adjusted down from $7.00 to $6.00. The company does expect to save $37 million per year in interest expense. CFO Roxanne Warner on the conference call said they expect to pay down debt by $400 million in 2026. She has since said another $900 million will be paid down from the stock issuance proceeds. That should bring total interest-bearing debt down to around $5.2 billion. The guidance estimates 5% organic revenue growth.

The $6.00 2026 EPS guidance appears quite cautious to me. It also does not reflect improved pricing in the market as the tariffs fully start to impact competitors. Management has said they are not seeing a major improvement yet, so they appear to be conservative. It also reflects a normal tax rate in 2026 versus a few taxes paid in 2025.

Tailwinds

Whirlpool has a number of tailwinds in place or likely to emerge. These are listed below.

1. Tariffs

U.S. tariffs are a major factor for Whirlpool because North America is Whirlpool’s largest market, with over 65% of total sales, and the U.S. is the vast majority of that. Tariffs are likely to shift from a major headwind to an equally large tailwind during 2026. Whirlpool is the only major appliance manufacturer based in the U.S., so it is the only beneficiary in the industry. Whirlpool manufactures 80% of what it sells in the U.S., way more than any competitor. The benefit should be quite large once it kicks in.

As I mentioned earlier, many of their competitors frontloaded products into the U.S. to get ahead of the tariffs. Since tariffs didn’t go into full effect until later in the year, this continued well into the second half of the year. It created an oversupply of product, resulting in fewer sales for Whirlpool and lower prices. To make matters worse, tariffs also increased Whirlpool’s cost of components and some finished products by $300 million in 2025. The pricing pressure appears to have stabilized and is starting to move in Whirlpool’s favor, as indicated by CEO Bitzer’s comments in Whirlpool’s January 29, 2026, conference call.

“We observed a meaningful change in industry pricing and promotions after mid-December into the MLK holiday and the upcoming President's Day.”

Management also noted in the call that the oversupply of appliances has been depleted, and it is seeing reduced promotional intensity in the U.S.

Going forward, Whirlpool should have a major advantage over its competitors. They are paying tariffs currently of around 15% on the non-steel portion of their imports and 50% on the steel content. Whirlpool only pays tariffs on 20% of what it sells in the U.S. and sources 96% of its steel from the U.S. These 2 tariffs should give Whirlpool a huge cost advantage going forward. Management believes that the advantage will be 5-15%. At the current rate of tariffs, the advantage is at least 10%. Management believes that its competitors who dumped product in the U.S. are losing money and will be forced to adjust pricing upward. The full impact of tariffs hasn’t hit them yet.

The key here is that even if tariffs go away, Whirlpool will still benefit from not having to face competitors dumping product at low prices and paying $300 million for imports of components and finished goods. If tariffs stay, the dumping goes away, and Whirlpool has a big cost advantage over everyone. The swing from 2025 to 2027 should be quite large.

2. Housing Cycle

While the tariff situation is near-term, a housing cycle upturn is probably farther off. New and existing home purchases are a very large source of buyers for Whirlpool. Both markets have been at the bottom of the cycle in the U.S. since 2023 due to a major increase in home prices and a significant increase in long-term interest rates. There is currently a 4 million-home housing shortage in the U.S., according to realtor.com. Others estimate this shortage even higher. Many homeowners are holding back because they are paying much less than the current 6% market interest rate on their existing mortgages. If and when longer-term interest rates drop significantly, it will provide a major tailwind for Whirlpool. Take a look at 2021-2023 in the chart shown earlier for what that potential is. Earnings were 3-4x current earnings back then when rates were much lower. Covid also helped then, but this time, pent-up demand is as big as it has been in decades.

3. Cost Reductions

Management expects $150 million of cost reductions in 2026. This is on top of $200 million in 2025. The cuts in 2025 were overwhelmed by the tariff headwinds. Whirlpool recently announced 341 job cuts at its Amana, Iowa, plant. Whirlpool is using AI to improve factory efficiency. The biggest source of cuts is expected to come from vertical integration and automation in the factories. These reductions are likely to be higher in the second half of the year.

4. New Product Launches

CEO Bitzer, in the last conference call, stated that new product launches in 2025 were the most in a decade. They updated or initiated 30% of their products last year. That cost them in 2025 as they had to pay for floor space and ramp-ups. CEO Bitzer stated in the call,

“The new products that we launched in North America gained over 30% incremental flooring on a like-for-like basis.”

More floor space at retailers usually leads to higher sales.

5. Small Appliances Growth

Whirlpool’s small appliance segment is called SDA Global. It currently represents about 7% of sales and is growing much more rapidly organically than the rest of the company. CEO Bitzer stated the following about it in the last call.

“SDA Global continues to perform very well, achieving impressive net sales growth of approximately 10% year-over-year in the fourth quarter and approximately 9% on a full-year basis, driven by new product launches and strong direct-to-consumer business. Fourth quarter EBIT margins expanded 130 basis points year-over-year as a result of a favorable price mix. For the full year, the segment delivered a strong EBIT margin of 16% with 170 basis points of margin expansion year-over-year.”

6. Activist

Well-known activist David Tepper’s hedge fund, Appaloosa, LP, owns 4.9% of Whirlpool. On February 25, 2026, they sent a letter to the board indicating concern about the recent equity issuance, failure to take advantage of the tariffs, and management entrenchment. Appaloosa is large enough to enter a proxy contest or even try to take over Whirlpool. Whirlpool is only its tenth-largest holding.

7. Litigation

In November 2025, Whirlpool filed a complaint with the U.S. International Trade Commission against LG, Samsung, and GE (Haier), requesting an investigation into patent infringement related to the company’s low-profile microwave hood combination products.

Risks

As with all companies, there are a number of risks with Whirlpool stock.

1. Balance Sheet

The balance sheet, though improved, remains highly leveraged. The EBITDA to debt ratio after the expected debt paydown from the stock issuance will be just under 5x. Tangible net worth was negative $3.0 billion on December 31, 2025. The biggest issue here is a possible additional dividend cut if earnings don’t improve.

2. Recession

Whirlpool is a cyclical company that is susceptible to recessions. However, a recession may also lower long-term rates, which could significantly improve the housing industry, its largest customer base. Whirlpool may actually benefit from a recession if rates come down significantly.

3. Geographic Concentration

Whirlpool gets 65% of sales from North America, most of which is the U.S.

Valuation

Management at the March 2, 2026, Raymond James Conference stated that its midcycle EBIT expectation is 9%. Midcycle means a normal operating environment, which excludes the current cyclical lows and the prior COVID-era highs. After deducting interest at about $300 million a year (after paydowns from the stock issuance) and taxes at 20%, that leaves a profit margin of 5.7%. That profit margin on guided sales of $15.45 billion indicates a profit of $880 million, which is $12.35/share based on the current diluted shares outstanding. There is a good chance we will be at least mid-cycle in 2027 from a tariff reversal alone and assuming no improvement from the housing industry.

My valuations usually compare the subject company to its peers and then adjust for growth, leverage, and other factors. Finding peers was a bit difficult as there is no other U.S.-based major appliance manufacturer. The first 3 on the list below are U.S.-based large home goods manufacturers. The final three are an overseas direct peer (Electrolux), a domestic small appliance manufacturer (Hamilton Beach), and an overseas small appliance maker (SEB). I did not include Shark Ninja as it is growing too fast to be comparable and is not a direct competitor.

This peer group suffers from poor growth, resulting in well below market P/E ratios and prices to revenues. SEB SA’s growth in 2024 was primarily due to acquisitions. Its organic growth was near breakeven in 2025.

Whirlpool’s revenue growth has been below its peers, in part due to divestitures. But it is guiding for above-peer growth this year. Its profit margin has recently been below that of its peers, though it was well above in 2021 through 2023. Whirlpool is more leveraged than any peer shown, though at this point not concerningly so. Whirlpool is priced at half of its peers based on price to sales, but similar based on its P/E ratio. Based on the peers, I believe Whirlpool’s P/E Ratio of 11.3 is priced at the market. However, that assumes no catalysts. Whirlpool has way more catalysts going forward than any of its peers. In fact, the tariff reversal alone could move its earnings to mid-cycle, if not farther.

I previously estimated mid-cycle EPS at $12.35 based on management’s guidance of a mid-cycle EBIT margin of 9%. P/E ratios tend to contract somewhat as an industry moves upward in its cycle. Based on that, I believe a price target for mid-2027 is 10x the $12.35 EPS mid-cycle estimate. That equates to $123.50. That is more than double the closing price of $57.55 on March 10, 2026. If and when the housing market recovers, it could go much higher, perhaps another double.

Takeaway

Two items out of Whirlpool’s control (tariffs and a housing downturn) plus two they controlled (a dividend cut and dilutive secondary offering) have upset shareholders, resulting in a much lower stock price. The latter 2 are in the rearview mirror. Tariffs are poised to go from a major headwind to a major tailwind. The housing market, due to pent-up demand, will likely rally strongly if and when long-term interest rates come down.

This is an opportunity to buy a cyclical company at its trough and with catalysts in place for a major rebound. Whirlpool has significant earnings power as shown by EPS exceeding $16 during 2021-2023. Meanwhile, the balance sheet, though still leveraged, has been improved and shouldn’t be a concern for a while. I recommend a long position in Whirlpool, with an 18-month price target of $123.50. I don’t expect much recovery this quarter and perhaps next, but we should really start seeing it in the second half of this year. That also happens to be the seasonally stronger part of the year.

Another benefit is that Whirlpool may be one of the few countercyclical stocks right now, along with the homebuilders. Recessions or even economic weakness usually result in lower interest rates. Lower interest rates are needed to make housing more affordable.
